title: Simple Adirondack Side Table
summary: A slatted outdoor side table to pair with Adirondack chairs — a couple of 1x3s and 1x4s, exterior screws, one afternoon.
description:
This is the little table that belongs next to a pair of Adirondack chairs — somewhere to set a drink and a book. It's about the simplest outdoor build there is: a slatted frame, four legs, and a slatted top, all from 1x3 and 1x4 stock with the gaps left open so rain drains through instead of pooling on the top.

The only decision worth making up front is material and hardware. Build it from cedar or another rot-resistant species if it lives outside, and use exterior deck screws — plain steel screws rust and streak, especially in cedar, which bleeds dark stains around steel fasteners within a season. Stainless or coated exterior screws cost a little more and save the table's looks.

Joinery is forgiving: a countersink bit and screws with glue is the easy path, or pocket holes if you have a jig. Either way the top slats should overhang the frame slightly for a finished look and to shed water past the frame.

tools.note: Or a miter saw. All square crosscuts.
A countersink bit is the simplest way to join this; pocket holes are the alternative.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Frame and legs. Cedar or another rot-resistant species for outdoors.

materials.name: Exterior deck screws, 1-1/4"
materials.note: Stainless or coated — plain steel rusts and stains cedar.

materials.name: Exterior finish (optional)
materials.note: Or leave cedar to silver naturally.

steps.title: Build the frame
steps.body: Build the basic table frame from the 1x3s. The easiest joinery is a countersink bit with screws and glue; pocket-hole users can use 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws instead. Keep it square.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Attach the four legs to the outside of the frame. Keep them flush at the top and check that the table stands without a wobble before moving on.

steps.title: Attach the top slats
steps.body: Lay out the five top slats with even gaps between them so water drains through, and a slight overhang past the frame. Mark, predrill, and attach.

steps.title: Finish or leave raw
steps.body: Enjoy it as-is — cedar will silver to grey outdoors and last fine — or apply an exterior finish if you want to hold the color. Ease any sharp edges first.

images.alt: A small slatted outdoor Adirondack-style side table
